what_it_does: Documents the vllm-stack bash CLI wrapper: its command surface, what each command delegates to, why it exists, how to add a command, known gaps, and links to related operations notes.
observations: The CLI is a single bash file at ~/vllm-tests/vllm-stack, symlinked into ~/.local/bin/vllm-stack.
Commands cover lifecycle (start/stop/restart), status/health/ports, logs, local model listing, benchmarks, chat/embed model swaps, optional jina-v4 management, and boot enable/disable.
Most commands are thin wrappers around systemctl --user, journalctl --user, or scripts in ~/vllm-tests/.
The no-argument usage block at the top of the script is treated as the source of truth for the command surface.
Benchmark commands activate the ~/vllm-env/ virtualenv before running the corresponding bench*.py script.
swap-chat and swap-embed stop a service, rewrite the MODEL= line in the launcher, and restart, with a noted ~2-3 minute cold start.
Open gaps include no swap-jina-v4, no ollama register/deregister, no export-state, and no tab completion.
standouts: The repeated design rule: "Don't grow this into a tool; it's a surface, not an engine."
Model-swap commands are presented as the canonical way to change deployed chat/embed models.
The usage block is declared the source of truth, requiring manual synchronization with code branches.
An explicit "Open at this topic" section lists missing commands rather than hiding incompleteness.
conventions: Uses YAML frontmatter with type, title, date_started, and tags.
Uses Obsidian-style wikilinks such as [[systemd-services]] for cross-references.
Includes a hub backlink at the top: [[_operations-index|← Operations hub]].
Documents command surface in a monospace usage block before explaining sections.
Provides a "Why a <thing>" rationale section and an "Adding a <thing>" extension guide.
Ends with "Open at this topic" and "Connects to" sections.
concerns: The usage block and case branches must be kept in sync manually; there is no single source of truth enforcement.
Paths are hardcoded to the author's home directory, limiting portability.
jina-v4 is described as optional, yet the file notes its service is currently enabled for boot, which could confuse expectations.
Several useful commands are listed as missing, suggesting the CLI is incomplete for disaster recovery and GGUF lifecycle.
notes: This file is the operational reference for the vllm-stack bash CLI wrapper, mapping each subcommand to the underlying systemd, benchmark, and model-swap routines it delegates to.
questions: Is the symlink and script content versioned or only locally maintained?
What exact launcher file does swap-chat/swap-embed edit, and how is the MODEL= line identified?
How are benchmark flags documented and validated before being passed through?
Does status include disk thresholds or just raw du output?
What HF ID format is expected by swap commands?
How is the virtualenv activated inside the benchmark wrapper?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the file states it documents the CLI surface and rationale, and it does so.
apparent_themes: Operational ergonomics through a thin, stable command surface.
Delegation to existing tools rather than reimplementation.
Documenting known gaps as first-class open topics.
intention_signals: To give future operators and agents a named, reusable interface for routine vLLM substrate tasks so they do not have to rediscover the underlying systemctl, sed, journalctl, and venv activation sequences each time.
novelty: The self-aware "surface, not an engine" design rule and an enumerated open-command gap list embedded in the operational documentation.
contribution: A catalog of the vllm-stack command surface, its delegation targets, the design rationale for keeping the wrapper thin, and an explicit inventory of missing commands and future extensions.
summary_for_embedding: Operations document describing the vllm-stack bash CLI wrapper, its lifecycle, status, log, benchmark, model-swap, jina-v4, and boot commands, plus delegation patterns, design rationale, and known gaps.
intention_for_embedding: To provide a stable, documented operational surface for routine vLLM substrate management so agents and operators can invoke canonical commands without reconstructing underlying systemctl and script sequences.
